title: Hakea humilis
intro: Hakea humilis, commonly known as the low Hakea or low white needlewood, is a shrub native to the southwestern regions of Western Australia. The plant has been traditionally used in Australian medicine, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in the treatment of wounds and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Hakea humilis include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenes, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Hakea humilis is often prepared as an infusion, tincture, or topical cream, and is commonly used to treat skin irritations, wounds, and minor infections.
taxonomy: Hakea humilis is classified as a species within the family Proteaceae. This classification places it among the flowering plants, specifically within the order Proteales. The genus Hakea is a group of shrubs and small trees native to Australia, and Hakea humilis is one of its many species. Within the Proteaceae family, Hakea humilis is a member of the tribe Hakeinae, along with other species of Hakea. This classification highlights the plant's unique characteristics and its evolutionary relationships with other plants in the family.
